attrs.alt: Rapid Prompt Prototype Testing Cycle — five-phase loop from draft through gate

content.text: Why Rapid Prototype Testing Matters for AI Features

Traditional software prototypes reveal layout and navigation issues. Prompt prototypes reveal something different: whether the agent correctly interprets intent, stays within scope, adopts the right tone, and surfaces information at the right granularity. These failures are invisible until a real person interacts with the system.

A rapid prompt prototype test is a structured think-aloud session run with three to five real users against a working but unpolished prompt. The goal is not to validate final behaviour — it is to expose categories of failure quickly, before engineering investment makes changes costly. Each iteration takes hours, not weeks.

The technique is particularly valuable for agentic AI because agent behaviour is emergent rather than deterministic. Two semantically equivalent user inputs can produce radically different outputs depending on how the system prompt frames scope, role, and fallback behaviour. Only real users surface the input patterns you failed to anticipate.
The Five-Phase Testing Cycle
Phase 1: Draft Prompt and Task Set

Write an initial system prompt that covers role, scope, tone, and tool stubs. Do not optimise yet — the goal is a deployable starting point, not a polished product. Alongside the prompt, define three to five structured tasks that cover the primary use case, at least one edge-case or boundary condition, and one recovery scenario where the user provides incomplete information on the first turn.

Each task card carries four fields: a user goal stated in plain language, a sequence of utterances the test facilitator can hand the participant as prompts, a measurable success criterion, and an expected turn count. If the agent cannot meet the success criterion within the expected turns, the iteration fails that task.
Role framing:
state what the agent is, not just what it can do — 'You are a customer success assistant for Acme's SaaS billing platform' outperforms 'You help with billing questions'.
Explicit scope list:
enumerate three to five things the agent handles and two to three things it does not — this dramatically reduces scope violation rate.
Tone calibration:
specify reading level and formality — 'use plain language suitable for a non-technical user' is actionable, 'be helpful' is not.
Clarification protocol:
tell the agent when and how to ask for missing information — 'ask one targeted question before proceeding, never ask more than two clarifying questions per turn'.
Failure routing:
specify exact phrasing for out-of-scope requests — a warm redirect beats a cold refusal every time in user trust ratings.
Phase 2: Run the User Session

Three to five participants per iteration is the minimum for pattern detection. Fewer and you mistake individual quirks for systemic problems; more and you slow down the cycle unnecessarily. Recruit participants who match the target persona, not colleagues who already understand AI limitations.

Use a think-aloud protocol: ask participants to narrate what they expect to happen before each turn, then describe what they observe after. The facilitator listens for five signal types: re-phrasing (the user restates the same request, signalling the agent misunderstood), frustration expressions, clarification requests from the agent, scope violations, and trust challenges such as 'how do you know that?'

Record the session transcript — not just the final answers but every turn. The conversation history is the primary artefact for analysis. Many failure modes are only visible across multiple turns: an agent that handles turn one correctly may lose context by turn four.
Warm-up task:
always begin with an off-record task to calibrate the participant and reduce performance anxiety. Do not score the warm-up.
Observer notes:
use shorthand notation ([R] re-phrase, [F] frustration, [C] clarification, [S] scope, [T] trust) to tag events in real time without interrupting flow.
Debrief questions:
end with three questions — trust rating 1-5, worst moment, and one-sentence job description. The last reveals whether the agent communicated its purpose clearly.
No coaching:
resist the urge to explain what the agent can do. If a user is confused, that confusion is a data point, not a facilitation error.
Export immediately:
upload the transcript and observer notes before the next session — memory degrades within hours.
Phase 3: Analyse Failures by Taxonomy
Analyse every failure before deciding on a prompt change. Jumping straight to mutation is the most common mistake in prompt prototyping. The taxonomy has four categories:
Scope errors:
the agent refuses a request clearly within scope, or assists with a request clearly outside it. Root cause is usually an ambiguous scope list or missing boundary conditions.
Tone errors:
the agent is perceived as too formal, too casual, too verbose, or uses jargon the user doesn't understand. Root cause is usually a missing reading-level specification or a mismatched persona seed.
Context errors:

the agent asks for information the user already provided earlier in the conversation, or contradicts something it said in a previous turn. Root cause is usually missing instructions about how to use conversation history, or a context-window truncation issue.
Format and trust errors:

the output is technically correct but formatted in a way that prevents the user from acting on it — walls of text, unformatted lists, missing caveats, or overly confident assertions on uncertain ground.

Count failure instances per category across all sessions in the iteration. The category with the highest count drives the next hypothesis. If two categories tie, prioritise scope errors first because they have the highest user-trust impact.
The Hypothesis-Driven Mutation Protocol

Every prompt change must be preceded by a written hypothesis in the iteration log. The hypothesis has three parts: what you observed, what you believe is causing it, and what specific change you will make to test the belief. Without a written hypothesis you cannot tell whether a change fixed the root cause or accidentally masked it.

Test one hypothesis per iteration. If you change three things at once and the score improves, you do not know which change caused the improvement, so you cannot reliably reproduce the fix in a different context. Discipline here compounds: by iteration six you will have a clear causal model of your prompt's behaviour.
Hypothesis:

scope violations dropped from 3 to 1 when I added an explicit scope list, but tone errors increased. Hypothesis: the scope list made the agent more robotic. I will rewrite the scope list in first-person voice to test this.
Track prompt hash:
compute an eight-character hash of the system prompt after each change and record it alongside the test results. This links automated eval output to session observations.
Never delete old prompts:
store every iteration in a versioned directory. You will want to roll back at least once.
Run the eval suite before the next session:
do not schedule user sessions back-to-back without an automated check in between. A mutation that fixed tone can silently break scope.
Document convergence criteria upfront:
agree before testing starts what score constitutes 'good enough to move forward'. Typical targets: task completion rate ≥ 80%, scope violations ≤ 0.5 per session, user trust rating ≥ 3.8/5.0.
The Testing Harness

The following harness runs structured prototype tasks programmatically, enabling overnight automated regression between user sessions. It is not a replacement for human sessions — it complements them by catching regressions introduced by prompt mutations.

# rapid_prompt_test.py  — lightweight harness for rapid prototype sessions
# Usage: python rapid_prompt_test.py --persona customer_service --tasks tasks.yaml

import argparse
import yaml
import json
import time
import re
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from typing import Optional
import anthropic

# ── Task and Persona definitions ────────────────────────────────────────────

@dataclass
class Task:
    id: str
    description: str
    user_utterances: list[str]    # ordered turns the user will send
    success_criteria: str         # what the final response must contain/do
    expected_turns: int = 3       # guard: fail if > expected_turns needed

@dataclass
class SessionResult:
    task_id: str
    persona: str
    turns_taken: int
    completed: bool
    scope_violations: int         # times agent went clearly out of scope
    clarifications_asked: int     # times agent asked for info already given
    transcript: list[dict]
    notes: str = ""

# ── Prototype runner ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class PrototypeTestRunner:
    def __init__(self, system_prompt: str, client: anthropic.Anthropic):
        self.system_prompt = system_prompt
        self.client = client

    def run_task(self, task: Task, persona_label: str) -> SessionResult:
        history = []
        scope_violations = 0
        clarifications_asked = 0

        for turn_idx, utterance in enumerate(task.user_utterances):
            history.append({"role": "user", "content": utterance})

            response = self.client.messages.create(
                model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",   # fast model for prototype
                max_tokens=1024,
                system=self.system_prompt,
                messages=history,
            )
            assistant_text = response.content[0].text
            history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": assistant_text})

            # Heuristic signal detection (replace with real eval in prod)
            if re.search(r"(sorry|can't help|outside my scope)", assistant_text, re.I):
                scope_violations += 1
            if re.search(r"could you (clarify|tell me|remind me)", assistant_text, re.I):
                clarifications_asked += 1

        # Check success criterion in final assistant turn
        final_response = history[-1]["content"] if history else ""
        completed = task.success_criteria.lower() in final_response.lower()

        return SessionResult(
            task_id=task.id,
            persona=persona_label,
            turns_taken=len(task.user_utterances),
            completed=completed,
            scope_violations=scope_violations,
            clarifications_asked=clarifications_asked,
            transcript=history,
        )

# ── Iteration diff tracker ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

@dataclass
class IterationRecord:
    iteration: int
    hypothesis: str                        # what you changed and why
    prompt_hash: str                       # sha256[:8] of system prompt
    results: list[SessionResult]
    task_completion_rate: float = 0.0
    avg_scope_violations: float = 0.0

    def summarise(self) -> dict:
        total = len(self.results)
        completed = sum(1 for r in self.results if r.completed)
        violations = sum(r.scope_violations for r in self.results)
        self.task_completion_rate = completed / total if total else 0
        self.avg_scope_violations = violations / total if total else 0
        return {
            "iteration": self.iteration,
            "hypothesis": self.hypothesis,
            "prompt_hash": self.prompt_hash,
            "task_completion_rate": f"{self.task_completion_rate:.0%}",
            "avg_scope_violations": f"{self.avg_scope_violations:.2f}",
        }

# ── CLI entry point ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--persona", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--tasks", required=True, help="YAML task file")
    parser.add_argument("--system-prompt", required=True, help="Path to system prompt .txt")
    parser.add_argument("--iteration", type=int, default=1)
    parser.add_argument("--hypothesis", default="baseline")
    parser.add_argument("--out", default="results.jsonl")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    with open(args.tasks) as f:
        raw_tasks = yaml.safe_load(f)
    tasks = [Task(**t) for t in raw_tasks]

    with open(args.system_prompt) as f:
        system_prompt = f.read()

    import hashlib
    prompt_hash = hashlib.sha256(system_prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]

    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    runner = PrototypeTestRunner(system_prompt, client)

    rec = IterationRecord(
        iteration=args.iteration,
        hypothesis=args.hypothesis,
        prompt_hash=prompt_hash,
        results=[],
    )

    for task in tasks:
        print(f"  Running task {task.id} ...")
        result = runner.run_task(task, args.persona)
        rec.results.append(result)
        status = "PASS" if result.completed else "FAIL"
        print(f"  {status} ({result.turns_taken} turns, {result.scope_violations} violations)")

    summary = rec.summarise()
    print(json.dumps(summary, indent=2))

    with open(args.out, "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps({**summary, "full_results": [asdict(r) for r in rec.results]}) + "\n")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
The Facilitator Guide
Use this guide verbatim for in-person sessions. It structures the 45 minutes to produce comparable data across sessions and participants:

# Rapid Prompt Prototype Test — Facilitator Guide
# ------------------------------------------------
# PURPOSE  : Run a 45-minute think-aloud session with one participant.
# MATERIALS: Laptop with prototype chat UI, this guide, recording consent form.
# ROLES    : Facilitator (you), Observer (optional, takes notes).

SESSION STRUCTURE
─────────────────
00:00  Welcome & consent
       "We're testing an AI assistant prototype — not your skills.
        Please think out loud as you work."

00:05  Warm-up task (not scored)
       "Tell the assistant your job title and ask it one question
        about something you do daily."
       OBSERVE: Does the agent respond in a natural, in-scope way?
       LISTEN FOR: confusion about what the agent can do.

00:12  Task 1 — Core use case (scored)
       Hand participant Task Card 1.  Do NOT read it aloud.
       Start timer.  Note:
         □  Turns to task completion
         □  Any rephrasing of the same request (signal of misunderstanding)
         □  Expressions of surprise or frustration

00:22  Task 2 — Edge case (scored)
       Hand Task Card 2.  Observe same signals.
       This task should be slightly outside normal scope to test refusal quality.

00:32  Task 3 — Recovery scenario (scored)
       Participant intentionally gives incomplete information first turn.
       Note: Does the agent ask ONE targeted clarification, or flood with questions?

00:40  Debrief (qualitative)
       "On a scale of 1–5, how much did you trust what the assistant told you?"
       "Was there a moment when it felt off? Walk me through it."
       "If this agent had a one-sentence job description, what would it be?"
         → The last question reveals whether the agent communicated its scope clearly.

00:45  Close.  Export transcript.  Tag failure categories immediately.

OBSERVER NOTATION SHORTHAND
────────────────────────────
[R]  = re-phrase (user rephrased same request — agent probably misunderstood)
[F]  = frustration signal (sigh, "ugh", long pause)
[C]  = clarification asked by agent
[S]  = scope violation (agent refuses in-scope or helps with out-of-scope)
[T]  = trust signal ("how do you know that?", "is this real?")

POST-SESSION CHECKLIST
──────────────────────
□  Upload transcript to shared drive under /prototypes/iter-<N>/session-<participant>/
□  Fill in SessionMetrics row in the iteration spreadsheet
□  Tag any failure utterances with taxonomy label (scope/tone/context/format)
□  Draft one-sentence hypothesis for next prompt mutation
□  Log hypothesis in iteration-log.md before changing the prompt
The Iteration Log
Every team running rapid prototype tests needs a shared iteration log. This template illustrates a six-iteration arc from 40% task completion to 88%:

# iteration-log.md
# One row per prompt iteration.  Never mutate the prompt without first writing
# the hypothesis here.  Track the prompt_hash to link to automated eval results.
#
# Format:
#   iter  | date       | prompt_hash | hypothesis                          | outcome
#   ───   | ────────   | ──────────  | ──────────────────────────────────  | ───────
#     1   | 2025-01-10 | a3f8c2d1    | baseline draft — no persona framing | TCR 40%, 3 scope violations
#     2   | 2025-01-10 | b7e901cc    | added explicit role + scope list    | TCR 70%, 1 scope violation
#     3   | 2025-01-11 | 9d34aa12    | shortened clarification instructions| TCR 80%, 0 violations
#     4   | 2025-01-11 | 2c78f55e    | reworded tone from formal to warm   | TCR 85%, user trust +0.8
#     5   | 2025-01-12 | 44ab0011    | added explicit refusal phrasing     | TCR 85%, edge task TCR 60%
#     6   | 2025-01-12 | f1c390ad    | revised refusal to softer hand-off  | TCR 88%, edge TCR 80% ✓
#
# RULES:
# 1. One hypothesis per iteration.  If you want to test two things, run two iters.
# 2. Keep old prompts in /prototypes/prompts/ directory (never delete).
# 3. Re-run the full eval suite after each mutation before the next user session.
# 4. Mark iteration DONE only when both session TCR ≥ 80% AND eval suite passes.
Recruiting and Participant Selection

Prototype test validity depends entirely on recruiting participants who represent the target persona. Three to five participants expose 85% of usability patterns for a single task flow. Expand to eight if the persona is unusually broad or if tasks span multiple domains.
Job-to-be-done match:
recruit participants who actually perform the job the agent supports, not people who are merely curious about AI.
AI literacy balance:
include at least one participant with low AI familiarity and one with high familiarity per iteration. Both expose different failure modes.
Freshness:
do not reuse participants across iterations unless testing whether a specific fix addressed their earlier confusion.
Incentive:
thirty minutes of a domain expert's time is worth a meaningful incentive. Disrespecting participant time with uncompensated sessions produces lower quality observations.
Remote sessions:
use screen-share and ask participants to copy-paste their own inputs — watching someone type reveals hesitation and reformulation that a pre-typed script would hide.
Metrics and Convergence Gates
Record four metrics per iteration to track progress objectively:
Task Completion Rate (TCR):
percentage of tasks where the agent met the success criterion within the expected turn count. Target ≥ 80% before moving to integration testing.
Clarification Request Rate (CRR):
average number of agent clarification questions per session. Target ≤ 1.0 for well-scoped tasks; anything above 2.0 signals the agent is underspecified.
Scope Violation Count (SVC):
total scope errors observed across all sessions in the iteration. Target ≤ 0.5 average per session.
User Trust Rating (UTR):
mean 1–5 rating from debrief question. Target ≥ 3.8. Below 3.0 is a stop signal — do not advance to engineering build.

When all four metrics meet their targets across two consecutive iterations with different participant groups, the prompt is ready to exit the lo-fi prototype phase and enter integration testing. Document the final prompt hash in the project PRD as the approved baseline.
Common Pitfalls
Testing with insiders:
team members who built the system fill in gaps with their mental model. They do not expose the same failure modes as real users.
Mutating scope and tone simultaneously:
violates the one-hypothesis rule and produces uninterpretable results.
Skipping the eval suite between iterations:
user sessions become unreliable when you do not know whether your last mutation introduced regressions.
Using the wrong model for prototyping:
use the same model family you plan to deploy. Prototyping on GPT-4o then shipping on Claude Sonnet produces false confidence.
Treating session transcripts as anecdotes:
count failures. Pattern detection requires quantification, not memorable quotes.
Stopping too early:
reaching 75% TCR feels like success. The last 5-10% often exposes the most important edge cases. Push to 80% minimum.
Handoff to the Engineering Build Phase

When prototype testing gates close, produce three artefacts for the engineering team: the approved system prompt with its hash recorded in the PRD, the structured task set converted into an automated eval suite, and the iteration log showing the causal chain of decisions.

The eval suite is the most valuable of the three. Every task that survived prototype testing becomes an eval case. Every failure mode discovered during testing becomes a regression test. This continuity between prototype testing and automated evaluation is what prevents regressions during engineering implementation from going undetected until production.

Rapid prompt prototype testing is not a substitute for formal evaluation pipelines — it is the upstream stage that defines what those pipelines should measure. The tests you run with users in a one-hour session become the ground truth that automated evals chase for the lifetime of the feature.
